payload.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes Beyond Words as a single-player word-crafting puzzle/strategy game with roguelike run structure, modifiers, upgrades, board placement, combos, and boss-style puzzle challenges. However, the allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not contain a fitting pure word/puzzle/roguelike strategy archetype. The nearest computed candidate, hidden_object_puzzle, is not supported by the text: there is no hidden-object play, point-and-click investigation, or cursor-driven scene-search identity. Because the game's gameplay identity is clear but falls outside the permitted archetype set, classification should be rejected rather than forced into a misleading bucket.
payload.rejection_reason: Clear gameplay evidence exists, but it does not match any allowed archetype. The title is a word-based puzzle/roguelike strategy game, not a hidden-object puzzle or any other permitted taxonomy class.

payload.evidence_summary: Stored store text already established a first-person single-player horror thriller with tense exploration, supernatural threats, stealth, and pendulum-based environmental interaction. External evidence materially strengthens the classification: reviews and official pages consistently confirm no direct combat or weapons, puzzle-and-item-gated progression, a directed linear campaign across distinct island locations, and survival through hiding, sneaking, and evasion. That makes the game fit survival_horror more strongly than action_horror, with horror pressure driven by investigation, environmental puzzles, and threat avoidance rather than fighting.

research_payload.evidence_summary: Official store pages and the developer site consistently identify The Occultist as a first-person narrative horror/thriller built around exploration, puzzles, stealth, and a mystic pendulum rather than conventional combat. Post-launch critic reviews materially strengthen that picture by confirming no-weapon play, puzzle-and-item-gated progression, linear/location-based structure, and gradual acquisition/use of supernatural pendulum abilities. DLC evidence indicates only deluxe extras (soundtrack/artbook), not gameplay-expanding content.

core_loop: Explore the island of Godstone and its individual horror locations as Alan Rebels while investigating his father's disappearance.
Use the mystic pendulum to inspect anomalies, reveal hidden information, and manipulate parts of the environment.
Solve environmental puzzles and paranormal detective-style obstacles, often by finding items, clues, keys, or hidden objects.
Avoid or evade hostile supernatural entities to stay alive and continue the investigation/story progression.
perspective: Player controls Alan Rebels directly during exploration and investigation.
combat: Alan avoids direct combat rather than fighting enemies head-on.
Official store copy says he 'always avoids direct combat' and advances using stealth mechanics.
Critic reviews confirm there are no weapons or self-defense options; danger is handled by running, keeping distance, hiding, or sneaking past threats.
Hostile supernatural entities and pursuit sections are present, but combat is not the game's primary interaction model.
progression: Progression is primarily story-led and exploration-led, advancing by solving puzzles, gathering items, and unlocking new areas.
Alan's pendulum gains additional supernatural abilities over the course of the game; one review describes a total of four abilities.
Named abilities cited in reviews include seeing hidden phenomena, manipulating time, and commanding rats.
Collectible documents/hidden objects and notebook-based clue checking support investigation progress.
structure: Broadly linear progression, with critics specifically describing the game as linear even while moving across multiple locations on Godstone.
Advancement is frequently gated by puzzles, locked paths, and item requirements rather than open-ended sandbox systems.
The island contains multiple distinct locations/chapters of exploration, such as buildings and set-piece horror areas, but the overall campaign structure is directed rather than systemic.
modes: Offline 1-player support is listed on PlayStation Store; Steam lists Single-player as the feature set.
dlc_relationship: The released DLC/Deluxe Upgrade Pack requires the base game and upgrades it to the Deluxe Edition.
Its contents are non-gameplay extras: the complete soundtrack and a digital artbook with behind-the-scenes material.
No evidence found in the researched sources of campaign-expanding DLC, new modes, or gameplay-mechanical expansion content.

source_notes: Steam store page provided the clearest official gameplay description: first-person perspective, pendulum-driven interaction, stealth/no direct combat, puzzle/detective work, single-player feature, and DLC listings.
PlayStation Store corroborated the official gameplay pillars and explicitly listed 1-player/offline support.
DALOAR's official studio page confirmed the game's identity as a first-person narrative horror project from the developer.
GameSpew review confirmed released-game behavior: exploration-heavy survival-horror feel, no combat, puzzle-and-item obstacles, and multiple supernatural abilities used during investigation.

Gamesurf review added concrete progression/structure detail: linear adventure flow, pendulum-centered gameplay, hidden-object revelation, stealth against threats, no weapons, and four pendulum abilities acquired over time.
Steam Deluxe Upgrade Pack page established that the known DLC is cosmetic/supporting-media content only, not gameplay expansion.

payload.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame Samson as a single-player, story-driven open-world action game centered on close-quarters brawler combat and weaponized driving in a hostile city. Strong signals include 'Open World Districts,' 'Brawler Combat,' 'Cars as Weapons,' skill-tree upgrades, mission commitment via an action-point system, and a debt-driven narrative campaign. Although store metadata and prior candidate scoring over-index on racing due to the 'Racing' genre tag and driving language, the prose makes clear that driving supports a broader combat-and-story open-world action structure rather than defining the whole game as a racer.

This title is explicitly described as a story DLC for Starfield and its own copy is additive rather than contradictory: new locations, technologies, weapons, and combat against a military faction. Per DLC guidance, the parent game's gameplay identity is the primary evidence. Starfield's stored descriptions strongly indicate a Bethesda-style single-player open-world/semi-open sci-fi RPG with character creation, faction questing, dialogue-driven roleplay, exploration across planets, shooting/stealth combat, ship use, crafting, and outpost building. That aligns best with western_narrative_rpg, with immersive_sim_action as a secondary influence due to systemic exploration/problem-solving and stealth/shooter play.

Stored Steam/OpenCritic text and the external evidence align on a clear gameplay identity: Machine Child is a single-player girl-raising/nurturing simulation centered on choosing one of three daughters, guiding her through town activities, lessons, leisure, rumors, shops, seasonal trips, and branching story events. Progression is driven by skill/bond/affection growth and Tarot-based Inspiration choices, leading to 22 different endings. No combat loop is described; the game appears primarily menu/event driven with relationship-focused progression and replayable branching outcomes, which fits life_sim best.

research_payload.evidence_summary: Official store/publisher materials provide a clear gameplay identity for Machine Child: it is a single-player daughter-raising/nurturing simulation built around choosing one of three girls, guiding her through town-based activities and story events, developing skills and bonds, using Inspiration for Tarot effects, and branching into 22 endings. Steam and JAST give concrete feature details, and JAST Store clarifies the Steam restoration patch/DLC relationship. Independent external validation is still limited because the Metacritic listing had no critic reviews available when checked, so some presentation-level points (such as its interface-driven format and absence of combat) are inferred from official feature descriptions rather than explicitly labeled.

core_loop: Choose one of three daughters and raise her in the town of Maldia through lessons, leisure, shopping, rumors, trips, and story events.
Build the daughter's skills, bonds, and affection while making choices that alter her growth and available events.
Accumulate Inspiration and spend it on Tarot draws with unique effects that influence her path.
Replay for different event chains, unlockables, and one of 22 distinct endings.
perspective: Presentation is described as a screen/menu-driven nurturing sim built around a daughter home screen, story/event scenes, shop and rumor interactions, and collectible CG illustrations.
combat: No combat system is described in the official gameplay features; the advertised play centers on choices, stat/relationship growth, events, shopping, rumors, lessons, leisure activities, trips, and Tarot effects.
progression: Skills and bonds formed during play shape the daughter's destiny.
Affection changes her responses, including dynamic voice lines on the home screen.
Outfits are unlockable through boutiques/shops and story events.
Seasonal trips unlock commemorative illustrations, with five seasonal CGs per daughter plus a special CG.
Inspiration is a spendable resource used to draw Tarot cards with unique effects.
The game culminates in 22 Tarot-linked / Arcana-inspired endings.
structure: Single-run raising-sim structure with 700+ story events.
Begins with choosing among three girls: Florine, Cordelia, or Chloe.
Activity and event choices branch the run toward different Arcana paths and endings.
Set in the town of Maldia, with special events unlocked through town rumors and seasonal trips.
dlc_relationship: This is the base game, not DLC.
JAST Store offers a separate free item, 'Machine Child - Full Content DLC,' described as a restoration patch for the Steam version.
JAST says the patch restores content removed from the Steam release and is not needed for copies purchased from the JAST Store.

source_notes: Steam store page was the strongest gameplay source: it specifies 700+ story events, 22 endings, outfits, seasonal trips, Inspiration/Tarot mechanics, and single-player support.
JAST's official game page corroborates the loop and adds concrete system nouns: skills, bonds, town rumors, item shop, random events, lessons, leisure activities, and home-screen voice responses.

JAST Store's official DLC page materially informed the DLC relationship by stating that the free 'Full Content DLC' restores content removed from the Steam version and is unnecessary for JAST Store copies.
Metacritic was useful mainly as launch-context corroboration; at the time checked it showed the PC listing and no critic reviews yet, so independent third-party gameplay evidence remains thin.
